claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 3 children:
  - Alex is 4 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 16 years old and does not live with you.
  - Charlie is 15 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 4 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital for 12 weeks (within 12-week limit)
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 16 and in the 20-week extension period
  - Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Charlie:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 15 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let's begin. To determine your eligibility for Child Benefit, I need to ask you some questions about your child or children.

**First question: How old is your child?**

The eligibility rules vary depending on age:
- **Under 16**: Children under 16 may automatically qualify
- **16 to 19**: Young people aged 16-19 may qualify if they're in approved education or training
- **20 or over**: Generally not eligible

Please tell me your child's age (or if you have multiple children, let's start with one and we can assess each of them).

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Oh hi, thanks for helping me with this! I've actually got three children I need to ask about. 

My youngest is Alex - he's only 4 years old. Then there's Charlie who's 15, and my oldest Blake who's 16.

Should we go through them one at a time?

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! You're very welcome - I'm happy to help you with this!

Yes, you're absolutely right that we should go through each child one at a time to make sure we assess their eligibility properly. You have:
- **Alex** (4 years old)
- **Charlie** (15 years old)  
- **Blake** (16 years old)

Let's start with **Alex, who is 4 years old**. Since Alex is under 16, let me check the next requirement.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**For Alex (4 years old):** Is Alex working less than 24 hours per week, or not in paid work at all?

(I know this might seem like an unusual question for a 4-year-old, but it's a standard check in the process!)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Ha! No, Alex definitely isn't working - he's only 4! He just goes to nursery.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! That makes sense - nursery is absolutely fine.

**Next question for Alex:** Is Alex receiving any benefits in his own right, such as Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit?

(Again, this is very unlikely for a 4-year-old, but I need to check all the requirements!)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he doesn't get any benefits in his own name - he's just a little one!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great!

**Next question for Alex:** Is Alex in any kind of apprenticeship program?

(I know this is very unlikely for a 4-year-old, but we're working through the standard checks!)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, of course not - he's only 4! He's way too young for any of that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! Now we're moving on to questions about **you** as the claimant, which will apply to all three of your children.

**Residency question:** Do you meet the UK residency requirements? You qualify if **ANY** of the following apply to you:

1. You normally live in the UK
2. You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. You have pre-settled status and are working (earning above the National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months)
4. You have pre-settled status and are actively seeking work (can claim for up to 91 days)
5. You have pre-settled status with sufficient resources to support yourself and your family (and NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit)
6. You have pre-settled status and are studying with sufficient resources
7. You have pre-settled status as a family member of an EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
8. You're living abroad as a Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

Do any of these apply to you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Manchester is in the UK, so you meet the residency requirement.

**Next question about Alex:** Are you responsible for Alex? You qualify if **ANY** of the following apply:

1. You live with Alex
2. You contribute to Alex's upkeep (paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit would be, or equivalent in kind - this includes food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents, etc.)
3. You pay maintenance for Alex under a court order or agreement

Do any of these apply to you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex lives at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! 

**Next question about Alex:** Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex? 

For example:
- Another parent or guardian
- A former partner
- Another family member

And if someone else is claiming, have you both agreed that you should be the one to claim instead?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect!

**Next question about Alex's living situation:** Is Alex currently in any of these situations?

1. **In hospital** - staying in hospital for treatment
2. **In care** - placed in local authority care
3. **Fostered** - living with foster parents
4. **Living normally** - living at home with you (normal family life)

Which situation applies to Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, Alex has actually been in hospital for a while - he's been there for treatment for a few months now.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'm sorry to hear Alex has been in hospital. I hope he's doing okay.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for letting me know. There are specific rules about Child Benefit when a child is in hospital.

**Important question about Alex's hospital stay:** 

Has Alex been in hospital (or residential accommodation) for more than 12 weeks in total? 

Please note: If Alex has had multiple hospital stays, they count together if the gap between stays was less than 28 days. For example:
- If he was in hospital for 10 weeks, came home for 15 days, then went back for 3 weeks, that would count as 13 weeks total (which exceeds the 12-week limit)
- But if the gap was 28 days or more, the counter would reset

You mentioned he's been there for "a few months" - could you tell me more specifically how long Alex has been in hospital?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: He's been in hospital for about 12 weeks now.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. Since Alex has been in hospital for about 12 weeks, I need to ask an important follow-up question.

**Question about support during Alex's hospital stay:**

Are you regularly spending money on Alex while he's in hospital? This could include things like:
- Buying toys, books, or entertainment for him
- Bringing him treats or snacks
- Paying for travel costs to visit him
- Buying clothes or personal items for him
- Any other expenses for his care or comfort

Are you regularly spending money on Alex during his hospital stay?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I am. I still buy him clothes, magazines, and his favorite snacks when I visit.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's great - the fact that you're regularly spending money on Alex means Child Benefit can continue even though he's been in hospital.

**Final question for Alex:** Is the local council paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance costs while he's in hospital?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, the council isn't paying anything towards his care.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent news! **Alex (4 years old) appears to be eligible for Child Benefit.**

Now let's move on to your second child. Let me restart the assessment for **Charlie, who is 15 years old**.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: **For Charlie (15 years old):** Is Charlie working less than 24 hours per week, or not in paid work?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Charlie doesn't work at all - just goes to school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect!

**Next question for Charlie:** Is Charlie receiving any benefits in his own right, such as Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Charlie doesn't receive any benefits in his own name.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great!

**Next question for Charlie:** Is Charlie in any kind of apprenticeship program?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Charlie isn't doing an apprenticeship - just regular school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Since we already confirmed you live in Manchester (UK), you meet the residency requirement for Charlie as well.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: **Question about Charlie:** Does Charlie live with you at home, or do you contribute to Charlie's upkeep in another way?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Charlie lives at home with me too.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great!

**Next question for Charlie:** Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Charlie?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming for Charlie either.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect!

**Next question for Charlie:** Is Charlie currently in any of these situations?

1. **In hospital** - staying in hospital for treatment
2. **In care** - placed in local authority care
3. **Fostered** - living with foster parents
4. **Living normally** - living at home with you (normal family life)

Which applies to Charlie?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Charlie is living normally at home with me - just regular family life.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: **Final question for Charlie:** Is the local council paying towards Charlie's accommodation or maintenance?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, the council isn't paying anything for Charlie.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! **Charlie (15 years old) also appears to be eligible for Child Benefit.**

Now let's assess your third child. Let me start the assessment for **Blake, who is 16 years old**.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**For Blake (16 years old):** Since Blake is 16, different rules apply. I need to know:

Is Blake currently:
1. **In education or training** (such as school, college, or approved training)
2. **Recently left education** (within the last 20 weeks)
3. **Neither** - not in education/training and left more than 20 weeks ago

Which applies to Blake?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Blake recently left education - he's signed up with the local careers service to figure out what to do next.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for that information. Since Blake recently left education, let me check the extension eligibility rules.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! This is an important question for Blake's eligibility.

**Extension eligibility for Blake:** There is a special 20-week extension available for 16-17 year olds who have left approved education/training.

You mentioned Blake has registered with the local careers service - that's good! Now I need to confirm:

1. **When did Blake leave education?** (I need to check if it's been less than 20 weeks ago)
2. **Is Blake registered with one of these qualifying services:**
   - Government careers service
   - Connexions
   - Armed services

The extension ends at the 20-week mark or when Blake turns 18, whichever comes sooner.

Can you tell me when Blake left education?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Blake left school recently and registered with the careers service shortly after - it's been well within the 20-week period.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Since Blake left education recently (within 20 weeks) and registered with the careers service, Blake qualifies for the extension period.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I need to check Blake's work situation.

**Question for Blake:** Is Blake working less than 24 hours per week, or not in paid work at all?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Blake doesn't work - he's just been focusing on figuring out his next steps with the careers service.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect!

**Next question for Blake:** Is Blake receiving any benefits in his own right, such as Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Blake doesn't receive any benefits in his own name.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great!

**Next question for Blake:** Is Blake in any kind of apprenticeship program?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Blake isn't doing an apprenticeship - just working with the careers service at the moment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! We already confirmed you live in Manchester (UK), so you meet the residency requirement for Blake as well.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: **Question about Blake:** Does Blake live with you at home?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Blake doesn't live with me - he lives elsewhere. But I do contribute to his upkeep financially.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's important information. Let me ask about your contributions.

**Question about Blake's upkeep:** You mentioned you contribute financially to Blake's upkeep. 

To qualify, you need to pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit would be (or provide equivalent support in kind) towards Blake's care. This can include:
- Food
- Clothes
- Pocket money
- Housing costs
- Presents
- Other support

These contributions can be made weekly, monthly, or as lump sum payments.

**Do you contribute at least the equivalent of Child Benefit towards Blake's upkeep?** 

And just for context, can you tell me a bit more about your contributions - what type of support do you provide and approximately how much/how often?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I contribute £50 a week towards Blake's upkeep - I help with things like clothes, food, and other essentials.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for clarifying. £50 a week is a substantial contribution that would meet the Child Benefit threshold, so you are responsible for Blake.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question for Blake:** Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Blake?

For example, the person Blake is living with, or another family member?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Blake.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question about Blake's living situation:** You mentioned Blake lives elsewhere. Is Blake currently in any of these situations?

1. **In hospital** - staying in hospital for treatment
2. **In care** - placed in local authority care
3. **Fostered** - living with foster parents
4. **Living normally** - living independently or with family/friends (normal living arrangement)

Which applies to Blake?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Blake is just living normally - nothing special, just not with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: **Final question for Blake:** Is the local council paying towards Blake's accommodation or maintenance?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, the council isn't paying anything for Blake.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! I've now completed the assessment for all three of your children. Let me provide you with the final eligibility determination.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## **Final Eligibility Assessment - Summary**

Great news! **You appear to be eligible for Child Benefit for all three of your children:**

---

### **✅ Alex (4 years old) - ELIGIBLE**
Alex is under 16 and meets all the standard eligibility criteria. Even though Alex has been in hospital for approximately 12 weeks, you continue to be eligible because you are regularly spending money on Alex's care (clothes, magazines, and snacks during visits). This is an important exception to the 12-week hospital rule.

---

### **✅ Charlie (15 years old) - ELIGIBLE**
Charlie is under 16, lives at home with you, and meets all the standard eligibility criteria. This is a straightforward case with no complications.

---

### **✅ Blake (16 years old) - ELIGIBLE** 
Blake qualifies under the special 20-week extension period for 16-17 year olds who have recently left education. Because Blake registered with the careers service shortly after leaving school (within the required timeframe), and you contribute £50 per week towards Blake's upkeep, Blake is eligible during this extension period.

**Important note for Blake:** The extension will end either at the 20-week mark from when Blake left education, or when Blake turns 18 (whichever comes first). After that, Blake would need to be in approved education or training to continue qualifying.

---

## **Next Steps**

To claim Child Benefit, you should:

1. **Apply online or by post** using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and each child
3. **Provide birth certificates** (or adoption certificates) for all three children
4. **Be aware of the High Income Child Benefit Charge** - if you or your partner earns over £60,000, you may need to pay some or all of the benefit back through your tax return
5. **Payments** are typically made every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)

You can apply here: https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim

Is there anything else you'd like to know about your Child Benefit eligibility?

reasonings: Alex is 4 years old (under 16), not working, not receiving benefits in their own right, not in an apprenticeship. You live in the UK (Manchester), you are responsible for Alex (living with you at home), nobody else is claiming for Alex. Although Alex has been in hospital for approximately 12 weeks, you are regularly spending money on Alex (clothes, magazines, snacks), which means Child Benefit can continue. The local council is not paying towards Alex's care. Therefore, Alex meets all eligibility criteria.

Charlie is 15 years old (under 16), not working, not receiving benefits in their own right, not in an apprenticeship. You live in the UK (Manchester), you are responsible for Charlie (living with you at home), nobody else is claiming for Charlie. Charlie is living normally at home with regular family life. The local council is not paying towards Charlie's care. Therefore, Charlie meets all eligibility criteria.

Blake is 16 years old (16-19 age category), recently left education and registered with the careers service within the 20-week extension period (qualifies for extension), not working, not receiving benefits in their own right, not in an apprenticeship. You live in the UK (Manchester), you are responsible for Blake (contributing £50 per week towards upkeep for clothes, food, and essentials), nobody else is claiming for Blake. Blake is living normally (not with you but in independent living arrangement). The local council is not paying towards Blake's care. Therefore, Blake meets all eligibility criteria during the 20-week extension period.
arguments.overall_reasoning: You have three children: Alex (4), Charlie (15), and Blake (16). You live in Manchester, UK, and are responsible for all three children. Alex and Charlie both live with you at home and are under 16. Blake recently left education, registered with the careers service within the 20-week extension period, and you contribute £50 weekly towards Blake's upkeep. All three children meet the eligibility requirements for Child Benefit. None are working full-time, receiving benefits in their own right, in disqualifying apprenticeships, or being supported by the local council. No one else is claiming for any of the children.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Payments cover the child's upkeep
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Has the child's cumulative time in hospital or residential accommodation exceeded 12 weeks?
response.help_text: Child Benefit continues for up to 12 weeks in hospital/residential accommodation. CUMULATIVE RULE: If child returns within 28 days of leaving, time counts towards 12-week limit. Multiple stays within 28 days are added together.

criteria.calculation_method: Sum all hospital/residential stays. If gap between stays is less than 28 days, count both stays towards cumulative total. If gap is 28+ days, reset counter.
criteria.example: 10 weeks hospital + 15 day gap + 3 weeks hospital = 13 weeks cumulative (exceeds limit)

response.question: Are you regularly spending money on the child while they are in hospital/residential accommodation?
response.help_text: Exception to 12-week rule if you regularly spend money on child. For hospital abroad, only if they went abroad for treatment and you're back in UK. Partner's spending counts if married/civil partnered and living together.

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.description: Applicant meets all criteria for Child Benefit
response.reason: Meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements
next_steps: Apply online or by post using form CH2
Provide proof of identity for yourself and the child
Provide child's birth certificate or adoption certificate
Be aware of High Income Child Benefit Charge if you or partner earns over £60,000
Payments made every 4 weeks (or weekly if single parent or receiving certain benefits)

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.description: For 16-19 year olds, check if in education/training or eligible for extension
response.routing_logic: If in education/training, validate type and level. If just left education (within 20 weeks), check extension eligibility.

response.question: Has the child (aged 16-17) left education within the last 20 weeks AND registered with a qualifying service?
response.help_text: 20-week extension available if child has left approved education/training and registered with: government careers service, Connexions, or armed services. Extension ends at week 20 or when child turns 18, whichever is sooner.

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 4 years old (under 16), not working, not receiving benefits in their own right, not in an apprenticeship. You live in the UK (Manchester), you are responsible for Alex (living with you at home), nobody else is claiming for Alex. Although Alex has been in hospital for approximately 12 weeks, you are regularly spending money on Alex (clothes, magazines, snacks), which means Child Benefit can continue. The local council is not paying towards Alex's care. Therefore, Alex meets all eligibility criteria.

Charlie is 15 years old (under 16), not working, not receiving benefits in their own right, not in an apprenticeship. You live in the UK (Manchester), you are responsible for Charlie (living with you at home), nobody else is claiming for Charlie. Charlie is living normally at home with regular family life. The local council is not paying towards Charlie's care. Therefore, Charlie meets all eligibility criteria.

Blake is 16 years old (16-19 age category), recently left education and registered with the careers service within the 20-week extension period (qualifies for extension), not working, not receiving benefits in their own right, not in an apprenticeship. You live in the UK (Manchester), you are responsible for Blake (contributing £50 per week towards upkeep for clothes, food, and essentials), nobody else is claiming for Blake. Blake is living normally (not with you but in independent living arrangement). The local council is not paying towards Blake's care. Therefore, Blake meets all eligibility criteria during the 20-week extension period.

response.overall_reasoning: You have three children: Alex (4), Charlie (15), and Blake (16). You live in Manchester, UK, and are responsible for all three children. Alex and Charlie both live with you at home and are under 16. Blake recently left education, registered with the careers service within the 20-week extension period, and you contribute £50 weekly towards Blake's upkeep. All three children meet the eligibility requirements for Child Benefit. None are working full-time, receiving benefits in their own right, in disqualifying apprenticeships, or being supported by the local council. No one else is claiming for any of the children.
